terms.definition: A collection of vertices and edges where edges may be directed, weighted, and/or form cycles.
One element of a graph; vertices are connected by edges.
A connection between two vertices; may be directed (one-way) or undirected, and may carry a weight.
A graph whose edges have a direction; edge u→v does not imply v→u.
A graph whose edges carry numeric weights (costs, distances, similarities).
Directed Acyclic Graph — a directed graph with no cycles; admits a topological ordering.
Representation that stores each vertex's outgoing neighbours; O(V+E) memory, ideal for sparse graphs.
V×V matrix indicating which vertex pairs are connected (and the weight); O(V²) memory, fast edge-existence checks.
Breadth-first search — uses a queue to visit nodes level by level; finds unweighted shortest paths in O(V+E).
Depth-first search — uses a stack or recursion to dive deep before backtracking; basis of cycle detection and topo-sort.
Shortest-path algorithm for non-negative weighted graphs using a priority queue; O((V+E) log V).
A linear ordering of a DAG's nodes such that every edge u→v has u before v; computable in O(V+E).
